title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: The concept of 'sustainable fashion' is a contradiction in terms. The fashion industry's business model is predicated on planned obsolescence and the constant creation of new 'needs.' Replacing traditional cotton with organic fibers does nothing to address the sheer volume of waste produced by a culture that views clothes as disposable. Real change will only come when we stop buying 'new' altogether and move toward a radical culture of repair and second-hand exchange. Anything else is just 'greenwashing' by corporations trying to soothe the conscience of the consumer.
texts.B: While I share the skepticism regarding corporate motives, we must recognize the power of 'incremental progress.' If every major retailer switched to recycled polyester and closed-loop water systems, the environmental impact would be massive. We shouldn't let the 'perfect' be the enemy of the 'good.' Furthermore, the fashion industry provides millions of jobs in developing nations. A total boycott would be an economic catastrophe for the very workers we claim to protect. We need to reform the supply chain, not destroy the industry.
texts.C: I agree that the volume of production is the real crisis, but I disagree that second-hand markets are a complete solution. Many second-hand clothes are simply exported to the Global South, where they destroy local textile industries. I share the view that we need to prioritize 'durability.' We should move toward a 'rental' model for high-fashion and a 'subscription' model for basics, where the manufacturer retains ownership and responsibility for the garment's end-of-life. This aligns profit with longevity, rather than disposability.
texts.D: The focus on 'materials' and 'waste' ignores the most powerful tool for change: transparency. If every garment came with a 'digital passport' showing its full carbon footprint and the wages of its makers, consumers would naturally gravitate toward ethical choices. While I share the view that 'fast fashion' is a disaster, I believe the market is already shifting. Gen Z is more interested in 'vintage' and 'upcycling' than any previous generation. The solution is not more regulation, but more data and better consumer education.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the effectiveness of corporate sustainability initiatives?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls corporate efforts 'greenwashing' and ineffective. Expert B argues for 'incremental progress' and claims the impact of retailers switching to better materials would be 'massive.'

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the fundamental problem being the volume of production?
questions.explanation: Expert A focuses on the 'sheer volume of waste' and the culture of new 'needs.' Expert C explicitly agrees that 'the volume of production is the real crisis.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the changing habits of younger consumers?
questions.explanation: Expert D mentions Gen Z's interest in 'vintage' and 'upcycling.' Expert A also points toward a 'culture of repair and second-hand exchange' as the only way forward, aligning with those values.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the best way to handle the future of the fashion industry?
questions.explanation: Expert B wants to 'reform the supply chain' within the current model. Expert C proposes a 'radical' move to a 'rental' or 'subscription' model where ownership is fundamentally changed.
